The heatwave and your gut
Dr Shubham Jain, gastroenterologist, explains how rising April temperatures are disrupting digestion in Mumbai
The spike in temperatures across Mumbai this April has been abrupt, and the body has had little time to adjust. That stress does not stay on the surface. It shows up quickly in the gut. Digestion is one of the first systems to falter when the body is forced to prioritise cooling over everything else, and that shift explains why many people report nausea, heaviness, acidity or irregular bowel movements as the heat intensifies.
This pattern has little to do with the calendar and everything to do with physiology. Once temperatures climb past the mid-30s, blood flow is redirected towards the skin to release heat. That leaves the digestive tract under-resourced, slowing down the breakdown and movement of food. A meal that felt routine a week ago can now feel unusually heavy or leave you slightly nauseous after even mild activity.
Why dehydration disrupts the gut
Dehydration sits at the centre of most summer-related digestive complaints. As the body loses water through sweat, it also loses the fluid balance needed for smooth digestion. The intestines rely on adequate hydration to move food along efficiently. When that lubrication drops, constipation follows.
Acid reflux also tends to worsen in this state. Reduced hydration affects the stomach’s ability to regulate acid, which can lead to irritation and heartburn. Many patients assume a specific food is responsible, when in reality the underlying issue is insufficient fluid intake.
Nausea is another common outcome. Irregular eating patterns combined with dehydration can interfere with how the stomach processes food, creating that unsettled, uneasy feeling that often shows up in the middle of a hot day.
At the other end of the spectrum, loose motions become more frequent during heatwaves. High temperatures accelerate food spoilage, especially in street food or improperly stored meals, increasing the risk of gastrointestinal infections. Dehydration can further impair the intestine’s ability to absorb water, leading to watery stools.
Eating patterns that make it worse
Summer does not just change what people eat, it changes how they eat. Skipping meals, relying on sugary cold drinks, or eating heavy food during peak afternoon heat all add strain to an already stressed system.
Large meals in the middle of the day are particularly difficult to process. The body is already working hard to regulate temperature, and digestion becomes inefficient under that load. Late-night meals can also aggravate acidity and disrupt the gut’s rhythm.
Ice-cold drinks offer brief relief but can slow gastric activity and interfere with digestion. In contrast, room-temperature fluids and traditional options such as buttermilk, coconut water or nimbu pani support hydration without shocking the system. Alcohol and excessive caffeine deserve caution here. Both can irritate the stomach lining and aggravate reflux, particularly in hot weather when the body is already vulnerable to dehydration.
What helps
Managing gut health in a heatwave is less about restriction and more about timing and balance.
Hydration remains the first priority. Around three to four litres of water a day is a reasonable target for most adults in this climate, adjusted for activity levels. Foods with high water content such as watermelon, cucumber and oranges can support this. Meals should be lighter and more frequent. Options such as khichdi, curd rice, dalia, lauki, steamed idlis or fermented rice are easier to digest and place less demand on the system. Leafy vegetables, smoothies and simple dal-based meals also work well.
Timing matters. Breakfast between 8 am and 9 am, lunch by early afternoon, and dinner by 8 pm allows the body to digest food when temperatures are relatively lower. Heavy meals during peak heat hours are best avoided.
Physical activity should shift to early mornings or evenings, with strenuous exercise avoided in the afternoon. This reduces overall strain on the body and indirectly supports digestion. Food safety becomes critical in this weather. Freshly prepared meals are safer, while street food or items left exposed to heat carry a higher risk of infection.
When to pay attention
Digestive symptoms are often the first warning that the body is struggling with heat. Persistent nausea, vomiting, abdominal cramps, diarrhoea, or signs of dehydration such as dry mouth, dizziness and dark urine should not be ignored.
These are not minor inconveniences. They are early signals that the system is under stress and may be heading towards more serious heat-related illness if left unchecked.
The broader point is simple. The body needs time to adapt to rising temperatures, and digestion reflects that adjustment. Light meals, steady hydration and sensible timing go a long way in preventing avoidable discomfort. Ignore those signals, and the gut is usually the first place where the consequences appear.
The author is a Consultant Gastroenterologist at Apollo Spectra Hospital
